title: The Moon — Origin, Characteristics, and Influence on Earth

blocks.text: The Moon is Earth's only natural satellite, orbiting at a mean distance of approximately 384,400 km. It has a diameter of 3,474 km (about one-quarter of Earth's), making it the fifth-largest moon in the Solar System and the largest relative to its host planet. The Moon's mass is about 1/81 of Earth's, and its surface gravity is one-sixth of Earth's (1.62 m/s²).

The Giant Impact Hypothesis (accepted model): Approximately 4.5 billion years ago, a Mars-sized body named Theia struck proto-Earth at an oblique angle. The collision melted both bodies partially, with the resulting debris disc orbiting Earth eventually coalescing to form the Moon. Evidence: (1) Moon's isotopic oxygen ratios match Earth's mantle precisely, (2) Moon has a disproportionately small iron core (~2% mass vs Earth's ~32%), (3) Moon rocks lack volatile elements consistent with formation in extreme heat.

The Moon's surface is divided into two terrain types: (1) Maria (singular: mare, 'seas') — dark, flat basaltic plains formed by ancient volcanic eruptions 3.1-3.9 billion years ago, covering ~16% of surface, concentrated on the near side. (2) Highlands (terrae) — lighter, heavily cratered ancient crust (anorthositic composition), representing the original solidified crust from the lunar magma ocean, approximately 4.4 billion years old.

The Moon is tidally locked to Earth — its rotation period equals its orbital period (27.3 days), meaning the same face always points toward Earth. This resulted from tidal interaction over billions of years. Tidal forces from Earth slowed the Moon's rotation until synchronisation was achieved. The far side of the Moon (often incorrectly called 'dark side') receives equal sunlight but is never visible from Earth.

The Moon generates tides on Earth through differential gravitational pull. The side of Earth facing the Moon experiences stronger pull (creating a tidal bulge), while the far side experiences weaker pull (creating an opposite bulge due to inertial effects). Spring tides occur when Sun-Moon-Earth align (new and full moon); neap tides occur when they are at right angles (quarter moons). Tidal range varies from near zero (Mediterranean) to 16 metres (Bay of Fundy).

The Moon is slowly receding from Earth at approximately 3.8 cm per year due to tidal energy transfer. Simultaneously, Earth's rotation is slowing (days are getting longer by ~2.3 milliseconds per century). 900 million years ago, a day was only about 18 hours long and the Moon was much closer. Eventually, both bodies will become mutually tidally locked — but the Sun will become a red giant before this completes.

The Moon's role in Earth's habitability is profound: (1) Stabilises axial tilt (23.5° ± 1.3°) preventing extreme climate oscillations. (2) Generates tides that create intertidal zones — unique habitats that may have facilitated the evolution of life from sea to land. (3) The giant impact that formed the Moon may have delivered additional material to Earth and accelerated Earth's rotation, enhancing the magnetic dynamo. (4) Moonlight governs biological rhythms in many organisms.

NCERT Class 6 — 'The Earth: Our Habitat', Chapter 1: 'The Moon is about 3,84,400 km from us. The Moon moves around the Earth in about 27 days. It takes exactly the same time to complete one spin. The Moon does not have conditions favourable for life. It has neither water nor air.' These basic facts form the foundation for more complex questions — UPSC tests integration of simple facts with advanced concepts.

NCERT Class 11 — Fundamentals of Physical Geography, Chapter 2: Mentions the Moon in context of Earth's origin. The textbook discusses how the Earth-Moon system formed from the same period of Solar System formation. Chapter 14 on 'Movements of Ocean Water' explains tidal mechanisms with Sun-Moon gravitational interaction creating spring and neap tide patterns.

NCERT Class 7 — Geography, Chapter 5 'Water': Explains tides as 'rhythmic rise and fall of ocean water twice in a day' caused by the gravitational force exerted by the Sun and Moon on Earth's surface. NCERT clearly states that the Moon's gravitational pull is the PRIMARY cause of tides, with the Sun playing a secondary role despite being more massive (because the Moon is much closer).

sections.title: Standard Reference Depth (Savindra Singh / GC Leong)

blocks.text: Savindra Singh discusses Moon origin theories chronologically: (1) Fission Hypothesis (George Darwin 1879) — Moon spun off from rapidly rotating Earth, leaving the Pacific Ocean basin. REJECTED: angular momentum calculations don't support it. (2) Capture Hypothesis — Moon formed elsewhere and was captured by Earth's gravity. REJECTED: capture mechanics unlikely without energy dissipation mechanism. (3) Co-accretion Hypothesis — Moon formed alongside Earth from same material. REJECTED: doesn't explain Moon's iron deficiency. (4) Giant Impact Hypothesis (Hartmann & Davis 1975) — ACCEPTED.

GC Leong covers tidal theory comprehensively: Equilibrium theory (Newton) treats tides as static ocean responding to gravitational forces. Dynamic theory (Laplace) accounts for ocean basin geometry, continental barriers, Coriolis effect, and resonance. Real tides are complex combinations of multiple harmonic constituents (M2, S2, K1, O1 etc.) creating the actual observed tidal patterns at any location.

Types of tides: (1) Semi-diurnal — two high and two low tides per day (most Atlantic coasts). (2) Diurnal — one high and one low per day (Gulf of Mexico, parts of Southeast Asia). (3) Mixed — unequal heights between successive tides (Pacific coasts). India's coasts are predominantly semi-diurnal with mixed characteristics in some regions.

Tidal energy potential: The Gulf of Khambhat (Gujarat) has India's highest tidal range (8-11 m) and is the most promising site for tidal energy generation in India. The Gulf of Kutch is another potential site. Globally, major tidal energy installations include La Rance (France, 240 MW), Sihwa Lake (South Korea, 254 MW), and proposed Severn Barrage (UK, 8640 MW). Tidal energy is renewable but geographically limited to high-range locations.

sections.title: Current Affairs Connected to This Topic

blocks.text: Chandrayaan-3 (ISRO, August 2023) achieved India's first successful lunar soft landing near the south pole (69°S latitude). The Vikram lander and Pragyan rover operated for one lunar day (14 Earth days). Key findings: sulphur confirmation at the surface, temperature profile measurements showing steep gradient in the top 8 cm of regolith, and plasma density measurements near the surface.

NASA's Artemis programme aims to return humans to the Moon by 2025-2026, establishing a permanent lunar presence. Artemis I (uncrewed, 2022) successfully tested the SLS/Orion system on a lunar flyby. The Lunar Gateway — a planned space station in lunar orbit — will serve as a staging point. India-US space cooperation under Artemis Accords (India signed June 2023) positions ISRO as a partner.

China's Chang'e-6 mission (June 2024) returned samples from the lunar far side for the first time in history — from the South Pole-Aitken Basin. These samples reveal ancient lunar interior composition and impact history. The far side crust is thicker and compositionally different from the near side, constraining Moon formation models.

Water ice at the lunar south pole (confirmed by Chandrayaan-1's Moon Impact Probe in 2008 and NASA's LCROSS in 2009) drives current exploration interest. Permanently shadowed craters at the poles may contain billions of tonnes of water ice — critical for future human settlements (drinking water, oxygen production, rocket fuel). This resource fundamentally changes the Moon's strategic and economic significance.

TRAP 1 — 'The far side of the Moon is always dark.' WRONG. It is called the 'far side,' not 'dark side.' It receives exactly the same amount of sunlight as the near side. It is simply never visible from Earth due to tidal locking. During a new moon, the far side is fully illuminated by the Sun while the near side faces away from it.

TRAP 2 — 'Tides are caused only by the Moon.' INCOMPLETE. Both Moon AND Sun cause tides. The Sun's tidal effect is approximately 46% of the Moon's. When they align (spring tides), effects add. When perpendicular (neap tides), they partially cancel. Stating 'Moon is the SOLE cause' is incorrect — it is the PRIMARY cause.

TRAP 3 — 'India was the first country to detect water on the Moon.' PARTIALLY CORRECT. India's Chandrayaan-1 Moon Impact Probe (2008) and the Moon Mineralogy Mapper (M3, a NASA instrument aboard Chandrayaan-1) confirmed water/hydroxyl on the lunar surface. However, spectroscopic hints existed earlier. India can be credited with definitive confirmation. The statement requires careful wording.

TRAP 4 — 'Spring tides occur in spring season.' WRONG. Spring tides have nothing to do with the spring season. They occur twice per month (new moon and full moon) when Sun, Moon, and Earth align. The term 'spring' refers to the water 'springing up' higher than normal, not to the season.

TRAP 5 — 'The Moon revolves around Earth in one month (30 days).' IMPRECISE. Sidereal period (relative to stars) = 27.3 days. Synodic period (full moon to full moon) = 29.5 days (because Earth also moves around the Sun, requiring extra time for the same Sun-Earth-Moon alignment). UPSC tests which period is which.
Q1 (2019): Spring tides occur when: (a) The Moon, Sun, and Earth are aligned (b) The Moon is in its first quarter (c) The Moon is farthest from Earth (d) Solar radiation is at its maximum

Q2 (2021): Consider the following about the Moon: 1. The Moon takes the same time to rotate on its axis as it takes to revolve around Earth. 2. The Moon has no atmosphere. 3. The Moon's gravitational pull is the primary cause of tides on Earth. Which are correct? (a) 1 and 2 only (b) 1 and 3 only (c) 2 and 3 only (d) 1, 2, and 3

Q3 (2023): With reference to Chandrayaan-3: 1. It landed near the lunar south pole. 2. India became the first country to successfully land near the Moon's south polar region. 3. The Pragyan rover uses solar power. Which are correct? (a) 1 and 2 only (b) 2 and 3 only (c) 1 and 3 only (d) 1, 2, and 3
Q1 (GS-1, 2017, 10 marks): 'Explain the origin of the Moon according to the Giant Impact Hypothesis. How does the Moon influence Earth's environment?'
Q2 (GS-1, 2020, 15 marks): 'Discuss the mechanism of ocean tides and their geographical significance. How can tidal energy be harnessed in India?'
Q3 (GS-3, 2023, 15 marks): 'Discuss the achievements of India's Chandrayaan programme and its significance for global lunar exploration.'

PRELIMS PRACTICE — Q1: Which of the following hypotheses for Moon's origin is currently accepted? (a) Fission Hypothesis by George Darwin (b) Capture Hypothesis (c) Co-accretion Hypothesis (d) Giant Impact Hypothesis

PRELIMS PRACTICE — Q2: Consider the following: 1. The Moon's near side has more maria than the far side. 2. Maria are younger than the lunar highlands. 3. The Moon has a significant global magnetic field. Which are correct? (a) 1 and 2 only (b) 2 and 3 only (c) 1 only (d) 1, 2, and 3

PRELIMS PRACTICE — Q3: Neap tides occur when: (a) The Moon is closest to Earth (b) The Sun and Moon are at right angles relative to Earth (c) The Moon is at its new phase (d) Earth is closest to the Sun
PRELIMS PRACTICE — Q4: India's most promising location for tidal energy is: (a) Chilika Lake, Odisha (b) Gulf of Khambhat, Gujarat (c) Sundarbans Delta, West Bengal (d) Palk Strait, Tamil Nadu
MAINS PRACTICE — Q1 (150 words): 'How does the Moon stabilise Earth's climate? What would happen if Earth had no large moon?'
MAINS PRACTICE — Q2 (250 words): 'Discuss the scientific objectives of India's Chandrayaan missions. How do they contribute to understanding lunar resources and formation history?'

PRELIMS PYQ ANSWERS: Q1 (2019): Answer (a) — Spring tides require Sun-Moon-Earth alignment (syzygy), occurring at new moon and full moon. Q2 (2021): Answer (d) — All three correct. Moon is tidally locked (same rotation and revolution period), has essentially no atmosphere (too low gravity to retain gases), and its gravity is the primary tidal force (Sun is secondary). Q3 (2023): Answer (d) — All correct. Chandrayaan-3 landed at ~69°S near south pole, India first to land in south polar region, Pragyan uses solar panels (which is why it shut down during lunar night).

PRACTICE ANSWERS: Q1: Answer (d) — Giant Impact Hypothesis is accepted. Fission: rejected (angular momentum). Capture: rejected (mechanics improbable). Co-accretion: rejected (doesn't explain iron deficiency). Q2: Answer (a) — Near side has more maria than far side (true), maria are 3.1-3.9 Ga vs highlands 4.4 Ga (younger, true). Moon has NO significant global magnetic field (had one ~4 Ga ago, now extinct). Q3: Answer (b) — Neap tides occur at quarter moons when Sun-Earth-Moon form a right angle. Q4: Answer (b) — Gulf of Khambhat has India's highest tidal range (8-11 m).

KEY FACTS FOR PRELIMS: (1) Moon distance: 384,400 km. (2) Sidereal period: 27.3 days, synodic: 29.5 days. (3) Giant Impact Hypothesis is ACCEPTED. (4) Spring tides = alignment, neap tides = right angles. (5) Far side ≠ dark side. (6) Moon has no global magnetic field NOW. (7) Chandrayaan-1 confirmed water (2008). (8) Gulf of Khambhat = India's best tidal energy site.

KEY FOCUS FOR MAINS: (1) Moon's role in habitability — tilt stabilisation, tides, impact history. (2) Comparative analysis: Giant Impact Hypothesis vs rejected theories (scientific methodology). (3) India's Chandrayaan programme — objectives, achievements, international standing. (4) Tidal energy in India — potential and limitations. (5) Lunar resources and geopolitics of space exploration (Artemis Accords, India's position).
